what_it_does: Documents a proposed one-line diff for runtime/suite.py that removes the raw scheme:// address from the operator-facing text of an R2 how-to context item while leaving the address in the sibling programmatic "address" field.
observations: Identifies the leak at runtime/suite.py:3738 inside Suite._r2_howto_at
The raw address is embedded in the composed text prefix "[how-to @ {address}]"
The address is already available in item["address"], making the text embedding redundant and a leak
Cites operator-law: Tim must never see code/files/machine-names
Proposes either dropping "@ {address}" or substituting runtime.territory.territory_label(address) for a human locator
Notes the change is in the R2 composer, not the run_swarm firing path, so no R13 byte-identical concern
Includes verify-after-apply steps: GET /api/context, check item["address"] remains, and sweep operator-facing text for scheme://
Marked as fabric-derived fork-authored PROPOSED diff for a shared/co-scoped file, to be applied by the suite.py owner/lead
standouts: Operator-law principle that raw addresses must not reach operator-facing text
One-line composed-prefix fix with zero loss of programmatic traceability
Explicit shared-file governance: fork proposes, owner/lead applies
conventions: Patch proposal format with trust/author/date/found-by metadata
Exact file:line citation for the leak
Use of bracketed channel IDs like ch-8djrpmsl and ch-piffgfxv
concerns: The diff is proposed but not yet applied
Assumes no downstream consumer parses the "[how-to @ ...]" text prefix
Verification steps reference runtime endpoints and fields outside this file
Optional territory_label alternative is mentioned without showing its import or behavior
notes: A fork-authored patch proposal to fix an operator-law leak in a shared hot file by removing a raw address from rendered how-to context text.
questions: Who or what is 'Tim' and how is operator-law enforced?
What is the R13 byte-identical concern referenced in the safety section?
How does runtime.territory.territory_label(address) produce an address-safe human label?
What is the governance workflow for 'met-at-the-address' application by the suite.py owner/lead?
intention_signals: To stop raw machine addresses from surfacing to operators in how-to context items while preserving internal traceability, and to route the change through shared-file owner approval rather than unilateral edit.
novelty: Frames a one-line composed prefix as an operator-law leak and pairs it with a 'met-at-the-address' governance note for shared hot files.
contribution: Records a specific operator-law violation site, a minimal fix pattern, a verification checklist, and a governance note that shared hot files should be changed by their owner/lead rather than the proposing fork.
summary_for_embedding: A proposed patch document for runtime/suite.py that removes the raw address from the operator-facing text of R2 how-to context items while keeping it in the sibling address field.
intention_for_embedding: To document and propose a one-line operator-law fix for a shared hot file with verification steps and owner-application etiquette.
